In this course, we're going to begin to talk about the business negotiation lessons learned at the Chase Manhattan Bank-Rabri-Hastage negotiation. These four lessons are the power of tonality, how a decision maker hides their position with their choice of personal pronouns, how mirrors work on everyone, even someone guarding their information, and how to gain control of negotiations by taking over how each interaction ends. We'll go into these in more detail as we proceed. I wanted you to know about things that we haven't been talking about anywhere else that's especially in this course. Good negotiators versus great negotiators. Good negotiators go into situations knowing they have to be ready for possible surprises. But great negotiators aim to use their skills to reveal the surprises they are certain exist. Experience will have told them that it's best to enter the situation with multiple hypotheses. The first goal in any negotiation should be to gather and observe as much information as possible. Too often people enter into a negotiation and find it easier to stick with what they believe from the get-go. They are already biased and will ignore indicators that contradict their current conclusions. The rest of this course will go over how to avoid sticking to the assumptions that put blinders on you and teach you how to uncover information by using techniques like mirroring, silences, and the late night FM DJ voice. By the end, you'll be a master at making the counterpart feel safe enough to reveal themselves and to discern what's really driving them. To achieve mastery of daily negotiation, you must first get over your version to negotiation. Most people hear the word negotiation and their stomach twists into a knot. They're thinking about they have to grind someone down or destroy the other side. But we're not talking about grinding someone down or completely destroying the other side. You don't need to do that. And in the long run, it's kind of productive. We are talking about recognizing the realities of human nature. Just like the book, this virtual course is about helping you excel at negotiation. You'll learn to use your emotions, instincts, and insights in any encounter to connect better with others, influence them, and get more. A successful hostage negotiator has to get everything he asks for and do so in a way that leaves the counterpart feeling respected because in hostage negotiation, believe it or not, we have repeat customers. So whether you're negotiating for a better raise or with a kidnapper, you don't win by splitting the difference. Just to highlight what I mean by having trouble processing information, try to memorize this pattern. Alright, what order did the colors of each icon appear in?
